QF.id: Duplicate Share Certificates and Transmission of Securities_23

question.text: Raman, aged 55, receives dividend income from his physical share holdings. Which form should he submit to avoid TDS deduction on dividends?

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** Form 15G is used by individuals below 60 years of age to declare that their total income is below the taxable limit, requesting no TDS deduction on dividends. Since Raman is 55 years old (below 60), he should use Form 15G. Form 15H is for senior citizens aged 60 years and above.
**The Trap:** The age cut-off is 60 years — not 65 years as some candidates mistakenly recall. At 55, Raman clearly falls in the Form 15G category. Form 15H would be applicable if he were 60 or older.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Form ISR-1 is the common KYC form for physical holders, and Form SH-13 is for nomination registration. Neither relates to TDS on dividends. The 15G/15H distinction is purely age-based (below 60 vs 60 and above).
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Remind investors to submit Forms 15G/15H at the beginning of each financial year. These forms are valid only for one year, and late submission means TDS will already have been deducted on interim dividends.
